skill.name: canonical-format-checker
skill.description: Checks documentation and lessons against authoritative source formats so teams can prevent drift in skills, agents, ADRs, PHRs, specifications, and command documentation.
skill.summary: Verify taught formats against canonical project sources before publishing.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and weak cryptography hits, but review found no executable scripts, network calls, credential access, or prompt injection attempt in SKILL.md. The weak cryptography findings are false positives from markdown text and file extensions, while the real risk is that the skill instructs agents to read local project files using shell commands for legitimate format verification.

medium_findings.title: Read-Only Local File Inspection Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the assistant to read canonical project sources before reviewing content and shows shell examples using ls, cat, and head. This is aligned with the skill purpose, but it can expose local project content if users apply it without scoping the review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains explicit read and shell-command guidance, but commands are read-only and target project documentation paths. I found no evidence of destructive behavior, networking, credential harvesting, or obfuscation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the referenced lines contain markdown frontmatter, file names, and documentation examples such as SKILL.md and README.md. No cryptographic operation or hash implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is plain markdown about documentation formats and filenames. I did not find code that invokes MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or any other weak cryptographic primitive.

low_findings.title: Inline Backtick Static Hits Are Mostly Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: Many external command detections occur on inline backticked paths, filenames, and example output blocks. These are documentation references, not executable code embedded in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are markdown examples and checklists rather than runnable scripts. A smaller subset of shell examples remains captured separately as a medium risk because agents may execute them during normal use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples for Local Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes bash examples using ls, cat, and head to inspect canonical source files. These commands are read-only and contextually legitimate, but marketplace users should know the skill may cause an agent to run local inspection commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and likely to be followed by an agent. The risk is bounded because they do not include user-controlled command construction, deletion, network access, or privilege escalation.

content.value_statement: Documentation can drift when teams teach formats from memory instead of source examples. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to compare content with canonical project files before publication.
actual_capabilities: Identifies whether content teaches skills, subagents, ADRs, PHRs, specifications, or commands.
Maps each taught pattern to a stated canonical source location.
Guides review against required structure, filenames, frontmatter, and section patterns.
Produces clear drift reports with observed issue, canonical format, and required fix.
Confirms compliant content with a concise verification checklist.
limitations: Requires access to the project files that contain canonical examples.
Does not validate formats that have no defined canonical source.
Uses documentation review guidance, not an automated parser or test runner.
May require careful scoping before reading private project files.

use_cases.title: Review Training Lessons
use_cases.description: Check that a lesson teaches the same skill, agent, or command format used by the project source.

use_cases.title: Validate Documentation Updates
use_cases.description: Compare new documentation with canonical examples before merging changes that describe platform patterns.

use_cases.title: Audit AI-Generated Guidance
use_cases.description: Find places where generated content invented a format instead of following the existing project convention.

prompt_templates.title: Check One Format
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use canonical-format-checker to review this section for skill format drift. Identify the canonical source and list any required corrections.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Lesson Against Source
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this lesson with canonical-format-checker. First identify every platform pattern it teaches, then compare each one with the matching canonical source.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Merge Feedback
prompt_templates.prompt: Use canonical-format-checker to produce merge feedback for this documentation change. Separate confirmed drift, compliant sections, and exact fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Multiple Pattern Types
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a canonical-format-checker audit across these files. Check skills, agents, ADRs, PHRs, specifications, and commands, then prioritize the highest-impact drift.

output_examples.input: A lesson shows a skill as a single markdown file under the skills directory.
output_examples.output: Format drift detected. The taught structure uses a flat file, while the canonical skill format uses a named directory containing SKILL.md. Update the lesson to show the directory structure and required frontmatter.

output_examples.input: A documentation page describes an agent without required frontmatter fields.
output_examples.output: Agent format drift detected. The page omits required frontmatter fields shown by the canonical agent source. Add the missing metadata and keep the invocation guidance aligned with the source example.

output_examples.input: A command guide matches the canonical command structure and review checklist.
output_examples.output: Format verified. The command documentation matches the canonical source structure, includes clear phases, and does not introduce a competing format.

best_practices: Define the canonical source before editing or approving any format guidance.
Quote only the minimum source details needed to explain the format decision.
Report exact drift and exact fixes so authors can update content quickly.
anti_patterns: Do not approve format guidance based only on memory or older examples.
Do not invent a new structure when a canonical project source exists.
Do not read unrelated private files when the review only needs one source path.

faq.question: What does this skill check?
faq.answer: It checks whether documentation teaches project patterns that match authoritative source examples.

faq.question: Does it automatically parse every file?
faq.answer: No. It guides a reviewer to inspect relevant canonical sources and compare them with the content.

faq.question: Can it review Claude Code skills?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes skill directory structure, SKILL.md naming, and required frontmatter in its review checklist.

faq.question: Why is filesystem access listed as a risk?
faq.answer: The skill asks the assistant to read local canonical files. Users should scope reviews to relevant project paths.

faq.question: Does it make changes automatically?
faq.answer: No. It reports drift and suggests specific fixes, but implementation depends on the user request.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it before publishing lessons or documentation that teach reusable platform formats.
